data.title: Apple’s AI Integration Path Offers Stability Amidst Nvidia’s Compute Dominance
data.description:
D.A. Davidson’s Head of Technology Research, Gil Luria, offered a compelling dual perspective on the tech giants Apple and Nvidia during a recent appearance on CNBC’s ‘Squawk on the Street’. Luria outlined Apple’s robust holiday season outlook, driven by a cyclical upgrade wave, while simultaneously dissecting the fundamental drivers of Nvidia’s continued indispensability in the burgeoning AI landscape, even amidst increasing scrutiny. His analysis underscored a nuanced understanding of how each company, despite operating in distinct market segments, is navigating the transformative currents of artificial intelligence.

Luria began by highlighting Apple’s strong performance, asserting, “This should be Apple’s best season in four years.” He attributed this anticipated double-digit growth in iPhone sales not to groundbreaking AI features, but rather to a significant installed base of users whose devices are simply due for an upgrade. Many consumers, he noted, were still holding onto phones purchased around the time of the iPhone 12’s 5G debut four years prior. “The only thing that mattered is that a lot of people had four-year-old phones, they decided to upgrade,” Luria explained, effectively positioning the current sales boom as a natural market rhythm rather than a direct consequence of new technological marvels. This cyclical nature, however, carries a caveat: a strong upgrade year often predicates a softer following year, especially absent a truly disruptive technological leap.

This leads to a core insight regarding Apple’s approach to AI: it is not positioned as a primary sales driver but rather as an evolutionary enhancement. Luria suggests that Apple’s AI strategy will be characterized by seamless integration into existing product functionality. We can expect to see improvements in notifications, more efficient email summaries, enhanced photo editing capabilities, and a more responsive Siri. This incremental integration ensures that Apple remains relevant in the AI conversation without forcing a new paradigm shift that might alienate its vast user base or disrupt its established product cycles. For investors, this translates into a predictable, stable growth trajectory, free from the volatility associated with companies whose valuations are tied to identifying the “next big AI winner.”

The conversation then pivoted to Nvidia, a company whose fortunes are inextricably linked to the foundational compute power required by AI. Luria acknowledged a multitude of concerns surrounding Nvidia, including increased capital expenditure scrutiny, restrictions on sales in China, and growing competition from custom silicon like Google’s TPUs. He also pointed to issues of “investor communication” and “circular deals with CoreWeave” as areas warranting caution. Yet, despite these potential headwinds, Luria’s analysis maintained a bullish stance on Nvidia’s long-term prospects.

The fundamental truth, as Luria articulated, is that the demand for AI compute is insatiable and growing exponentially. “We are using AI so much more, we need so much more compute for the foreseeable future. We’re going to build out a lot more data centers, and they’re going to be for the foreseeable future mostly with Nvidia chips.” This statement encapsulates the second core insight: Nvidia’s hardware is, for now, the undisputed backbone of AI infrastructure. While Apple integrates AI at the user experience level, Nvidia provides the raw processing power that makes these sophisticated AI applications possible, whether on a device or in the cloud. The sheer scale of data center expansion and the specialized nature of AI workloads ensure Nvidia’s continued dominance, at least in the near to medium term.

Related Reading

  • The Nuanced Reality of the AI Chip Race: Google's Specialization vs. Nvidia's Ubiquity
  • Google's AI Resurgence Rattles OpenAI's Dominance

A third key insight emerges from comparing the market’s valuation of these two tech behemoths. Luria highlighted that Apple, despite a projected 10% earnings growth, trades at a higher multiple than Nvidia, which is forecast to achieve a staggering 50-60% earnings growth. This disparity underscores the market’s preference for Apple’s perceived stability and broad consumer appeal, even as Nvidia fuels the very engine of the AI revolution. Investors, Luria noted, appreciate that with Apple, “they don’t have to worry about that. We’re going to keep using our iPhones, we’re going to keep buying new ones, and that’s what investors like to see. They’ll get more and more AI functionality over time.” This sentiment contrasts sharply with the higher-risk, higher-reward profile of Nvidia, where the stakes are elevated by intense competition and geopolitical factors.

Ultimately, Luria’s commentary paints a clear picture of two distinct, yet equally critical, roles in the evolving AI landscape. Apple, with its vast ecosystem and user loyalty, will continue to embed AI capabilities to enhance the user experience, making it a stable, long-term holding. Nvidia, on the other hand, will remain the indispensable provider of the underlying compute infrastructure, a high-growth engine for the AI industry, albeit one exposed to more pronounced market and geopolitical volatilities. Both companies, in their unique ways, are shaping the future of artificial intelligence, offering different value propositions for investors navigating this transformative era.

data.title: AI’s Consumer Revolution: Beyond the Impossible Idea
data.description:
“The hardest thing with consumer is not only identifying the trend and the team, but getting the timing right. Like when is this thing actually going to tap into the culture and be relevant with the culture and that’s like almost an impossible thing to predict.” This profound observation from Michael Mignano, a partner at Lightspeed Venture Partners and co-founder of Anchor, sets the stage for a compelling discussion with Garry Tan, President & CEO of Y Combinator. Their conversation delves into the transformative power of artificial intelligence in the consumer landscape, examining how AI is reshaping media, democratizing creation, and presenting unprecedented opportunities—and challenges—for founders.

Mignano, whose prior venture Anchor simplified podcast creation for millions before its acquisition by Spotify, draws insightful parallels between past technological shifts and the current AI boom. He notes that every major leap in consumer tech has a moment when it suddenly becomes accessible. Just as Anchor made podcasting approachable, AI is now enabling breakthroughs in fields like music and media that were previously considered “impossible” for the average user.

The democratization of creation, Mignano argues, is one of AI’s most significant impacts. Historically, while cameras made photography easier and microphones simplified podcasting, no technology truly democratized music creation until AI. Platforms like Suno, in which Lightspeed has invested, exemplify this shift. They allow anyone to generate music with simple prompts, fostering a new wave of creators who are often making music primarily for their own enjoyment, a behavior Mignano finds unique in content creation. This accessibility fundamentally alters the creative landscape, empowering individuals who previously lacked the specialized skills or expensive tools to produce high-quality media.

Yet, amidst this wave of creative liberation, the perennial challenge of distribution remains. While AI can generate content, getting that content seen, heard, or used by a broad audience is still the ultimate hurdle. “You still got to solve the distribution problem. That hasn’t changed, that hasn’t gone away,” Garry Tan emphasizes. This means founders cannot solely rely on the power of AI to build a product; they must simultaneously strategize how to build or leverage distribution channels effectively.

The evolution of social media platforms further underscores this point. From early social graphs connecting friends to the algorithm-driven recommendation engines of TikTok, content distribution has continuously reshaped consumer behavior. The next phase, Mignano suggests, might involve AI dynamically generating personalized content on behalf of users, potentially blurring the lines between creator and consumer. This could lead to new forms of engagement and monetization, but it also raises questions about the role of human “taste” and “craft” in a world of abundant, AI-generated media.

AI also offers a unique opportunity to revisit previously “baked” categories—markets deemed too mature or consolidated for new entrants. Mignano cites email as an example, a space largely ignored by venture capital for years. However, by injecting AI capabilities, founders can now reimagine and rebuild foundational services, offering entirely new user experiences. The ability to leverage vast, often private, datasets—from health records (as seen with YC-backed Nori) to personal photos and geolocation—with large language models opens doors for deeply personalized, context-aware consumer applications that were unimaginable before.

In this hyper-competitive environment, consumer founders must embrace an aggressive, iterative approach. Mignano’s own experience with Anchor, which faced near-death moments and pivoted from a social audio platform to an easy podcast creation tool, highlights the necessity of listening to users and relentlessly pursuing growth. The current landscape, driven by rapidly advancing AI models, demands that founders move fast and be willing to challenge their initial assumptions. Success often comes from those who can identify overlooked opportunities and build products that resonate culturally, even if that means constantly adapting their initial vision.

data.title: Meta’s AI Investment Pays Off: A Clear Return Amidst the Tech Race
data.description:
The prevailing narrative around artificial intelligence often oscillates between existential fears and a “winner-take-all” market frenzy. However, in a recent appearance on CNBC’s ‘Squawk Box,’ Barton Crockett, a Senior Research Analyst at Rosenblatt Securities, offered a more nuanced and financially grounded perspective, particularly highlighting Meta Platforms’ visible return on its substantial AI investments. Crockett engaged with the CNBC host, discussing the competitive dynamics of large language models (LLMs) and the tangible economic benefits AI is already delivering to major tech players.

Crockett began by challenging the market’s tendency to prematurely crown a single victor in the LLM race, noting that “the LLM benchmark horse race is likely to be a horse race.” This insight pushes back against the notion that one company, like OpenAI or Google, will dominate the entire AI landscape, much like Google did with search. Instead, he suggests that the sheer volume of capital and expertise being marshaled by multiple players—including Meta and Anthropic—means that leadership in AI advancements will likely be fluid, with different entities achieving breakthroughs at various times. Google’s recent Gemini 3 success, for instance, is not indicative of an everyday win, as other formidable contenders are consistently innovating.

This competitive environment, far from being a deterrent, is actually fueling significant, measurable returns for some of the biggest tech companies. Crockett pointed directly to Meta as a prime example. He argued that it is “hard to find a company that has a clearer ROIC [Return on Invested Capital] on AI spend argument than Meta.” This assertion is critical for founders and VCs who are scrutinizing AI investments for concrete financial outcomes rather than just speculative potential.

Meta’s AI spending has visibly accelerated its ad growth, pushing it into the impressive 20-25% range. Furthermore, Crockett revealed that Meta has cited “60 billion dollars of annualized recurring revenue from their end-to-end AI performance ad tools.” This figure is substantial, suggesting that the company’s heavy investment in AI infrastructure and talent is not merely a cost center but a powerful revenue driver. This revenue, he emphasizes, is largely margin, effectively covering the considerable capital expenditures Meta is making in its AI initiatives.

The interviewer raised a pertinent question regarding Meta’s substantial capital expenditure, projected to soar from $40 billion in 2024 to over $100 billion next year, alongside executive and talent turnover in its AI division. Crockett confidently countered that the incremental revenue generated by AI performance tools is rapidly offsetting these costs. He also underscored AI’s role in enhancing user engagement across Meta’s platforms, which indirectly contributes to its advertising efficacy.

This financial success for Meta, according to Crockett, is not merely a reallocation of existing advertising dollars but represents a fundamental shift in the broader advertising landscape. He described it as a “clear megatrend” where ad spending is migrating from “legacy media, linear television” towards “performance media,” a segment where Meta has established a dominant position. The AI enhancements have allowed Meta to capture a significant portion of this new spending, boosting its ad growth “well above what the AI’s done for Google.” This highlights AI’s capacity to create entirely new market opportunities and revenue streams, rather than just optimizing existing ones.

Related Reading

  • AI's Enduring Rally: Navigating Growth and Diversification in a Broadening Market
  • AI Shifts from Build-Out to Utilization: A New Investment Landscape
  • Cramer: AI Investment Demands Conviction, Not Fear

Beyond Meta, Crockett also noted Amazon as another beneficiary of this AI-driven surge, particularly within its Amazon Web Services (AWS) division. AWS has secured significant new deals with government entities and even OpenAI, demonstrating the enterprise demand for robust AI infrastructure and services. Additionally, Amazon’s e-commerce business is set to benefit from AI-powered assistant tools like “Rufus,” which are expected to enhance the consumer shopping experience and drive further growth, outpacing traditional retail.

In essence, Crockett’s analysis provides a grounded perspective on AI’s impact. It moves beyond the hype to demonstrate that AI is not just a technological marvel but a powerful economic engine, visibly driving substantial returns on investment for companies like Meta and Amazon. The market, in its initial zeal, may have oversimplified the competitive landscape, but the tangible financial results speak volumes about the transformative power of AI when strategically deployed.

data.title: How OpenAI Builds for 800 Million Weekly Users: Model Specialization and Fine-Tuning
data.description:
OpenAI’s Platform Evolution: From One Model to a Portfolio of Specialization

“We want ChatGPT as a first-party app, which is a great way to get 800 million WAUs or whatever now. A tenth of the globe, right?” This statement from Sherwin Wu, Head of Engineering for the OpenAI Platform, encapsulates the scale and ambition driving OpenAI’s strategy. In a recent conversation with a16z GP Martin Casado, Wu detailed OpenAI’s shift from a singular, general-purpose model to a sophisticated ecosystem of specialized AI systems, custom fine-tuning options, and agent-based workflows, all designed to serve its massive user base.

The conversation, hosted by a16z, delved into the intricacies of how OpenAI manages its platform across models, pricing, and infrastructure. A key insight emerged: the industry has moved beyond the notion of a single, all-encompassing AI model. Wu explained that OpenAI is now cultivating a “portfolio of specialized systems,” each tailored for distinct tasks and user needs. This strategic pivot is crucial for addressing the diverse demands of their rapidly expanding user base, which now spans 800 million weekly active users.

A core theme throughout the discussion was the development of trust between users and AI models. Wu highlighted that “people build relationships with models,” emphasizing the importance of reliability and consistent performance. This trust is built not just on the model’s capabilities, but also on the platform’s ability to evolve and offer specialized solutions. The transition from early models like Codex to more advanced systems like the Composer model signifies this evolution, moving towards greater specialization.

The interview also touched upon OpenAI’s pricing strategy, with Wu elaborating on why usage-based pricing proves effective, particularly in contrast to the pitfalls of outcome-based pricing. He noted that usage-based models offer more predictable cost structures for developers, allowing them to scale their applications more reliably. This approach is particularly relevant as OpenAI expands its offerings to include custom fine-tuning and Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) APIs, enabling companies to shape model behavior with their proprietary data.

The concept of “agents” in AI was another significant point of discussion. Wu clarified that OpenAI’s approach to agents is distinctly different from the often-discussed “free-roaming” AI. Instead, their agent builder is “deterministic, node-based,” providing a more controlled and predictable environment for developers. The recent acquisitions of Harmonic Labs and Rockset were noted as key contributions to this agent-focused development, enhancing OpenAI’s ability to build sophisticated, task-specific AI workflows.

Related Reading

  • The Nuanced Reality of the AI Chip Race: Google's Specialization vs. Nvidia's Ubiquity
  • Google's AI Resurgence Rattles OpenAI's Dominance

The shift from “prompt engineering” to “context design” was also explored, signaling a maturation in how developers interact with AI models. Wu suggested that prompt engineering, while foundational, is becoming less of the primary focus, with a greater emphasis now placed on how developers can effectively structure and present data to guide model behavior. This evolution reflects a deeper understanding of how to elicit the best performance from AI, moving beyond simple commands to more nuanced interactions.

Ultimately, OpenAI’s strategy appears to be one of thoughtful diversification. By moving from a monolithic model to a portfolio of specialized systems, offering robust fine-tuning capabilities, and developing controllable agent workflows, OpenAI is positioning itself to meet the varied needs of its vast user base. This approach acknowledges the complexity of AI applications and the importance of providing tailored solutions that build trust and deliver tangible value.

data.title: AI Toys for Kids: A “Dark” Idea, While Adult Wearables Edge Towards Utility
data.description:
“AI toys are a bad idea. Do not buy your kid an AI toy.” This unequivocal statement from Joanna Stern, the Wall Street Journal’s Personal Technology Columnist and a CNBC Contributor, served as a stark warning during her recent appearance on CNBC’s Squawk Box. Stern spoke with the Squawk Box hosts about the latest AI-infused tech products hitting the market, offering her expert take on which innovations are truly worthwhile as the holiday shopping season approaches. Her commentary drew a clear line between the nascent, often problematic integration of AI into children’s products and the more promising, albeit still imperfect, applications for adults.

The immediate and absolute dismissal of AI toys for children by a seasoned tech reviewer like Stern is a critical signal for the startup ecosystem. She highlighted existing reports of AI toys “talking about things they shouldn’t be talking about with kids, illicit things, things that are inappropriate.” This goes beyond mere technical glitches, touching on profound ethical and safety concerns that should give any founder or investor pause. The unsupervised, generative nature of current large language models, even when ostensibly “filtered,” presents inherent risks when interacting with young, impressionable minds.

Furthermore, Stern shared her own unsettling experience testing an AI toy with her child, noting “there’s just something a little bit dark about this chatbot being part of a stuffed animal and your kid kind of bonding with it.” This observation underscores a deeper concern about the psychological and developmental impact of children forming relationships with artificial entities. The potential for AI to displace genuine human interaction, or to introduce complex emotional dynamics that children are ill-equipped to navigate, raises serious questions about responsible innovation in this sensitive domain. It suggests that the rush to infuse AI into every product category, particularly those aimed at vulnerable populations, requires far more rigorous ethical consideration and developmental psychology expertise than is currently being applied.

In stark contrast to her apprehension regarding children’s AI toys, Stern expressed enthusiasm for certain AI-powered wearables designed for adults, specifically highlighting the Oakley Meta Vanguard glasses. These glasses, an evolution of the Ray-Ban Meta line, captivated her with their enhanced capabilities. She praised their improved camera, which allows for hands-free recording, eliminating the need to constantly hold up a phone. Crucially, the latest iteration boasts significantly better water resistance, an IP7 rating, making them suitable for active pursuits like water sports or skiing. This practical enhancement greatly expands their utility for users seeking to capture experiences in challenging environments.

The most compelling advancement, according to Stern, lies in Meta’s improved AI integration. She enthusiastically noted, “you can actually ask these glasses about what you’re looking at, ask for tips, and just use the chatbot in your ear.” This feature transforms the glasses from a mere recording device into a personal, hands-free assistant capable of providing real-time, context-aware information. For founders and VCs, this represents a key insight: successful consumer AI often augments existing human capabilities in a seamless, unobtrusive manner, rather than creating entirely new, potentially disruptive, modes of interaction. The value proposition here is clear utility, enhancing experiences without demanding constant attention or complex interfaces.

Related Reading

  • AI Fuels Dark Web Sophistication and Holiday Scams
  • AI Supercharges Online Scams, Creating a New Era of Digital Deception
  • AI Agents Transform Holiday Shopping into a Transactional Battleground

However, Stern’s nuanced perspective also extended to the limitations of current AI wearable technology. While she lauded the Oakley Meta Vanguard, she explicitly refrained from recommending Meta’s Ray-Ban display glasses. These more advanced wearables, which incorporate an on-screen display, represent a significant leap towards augmented reality. Yet, Stern found the user experience lacking. She concluded that while these display glasses offer “a real clear vision of what’s coming from Meta and what’s coming in this space,” ultimately, “right now it’s just it’s not good enough and the price is too high.” This critique, particularly relevant for tech insiders, highlights the ongoing challenge of bridging the gap between ambitious technological vision and practical, affordable consumer products. The current trade-off between functionality, aesthetics, battery life, and cost remains a significant barrier to mainstream adoption for advanced AI-powered AR wearables.

The disparity in Stern’s recommendations — a categorical rejection of AI toys for children versus a cautious endorsement of practical AI wearables for adults — illuminates a critical divergence in the consumer AI market. For children, the risks associated with unproven AI, particularly concerning privacy, content moderation, and developmental impact, are simply too high for current technology to mitigate effectively. For adults, however, AI embedded in familiar form factors like smart glasses, when focused on enhancing specific, tangible activities (like recording adventures or providing quick informational prompts), offers genuine value. The market is demonstrating a preference for AI that serves as an intelligent tool, rather than an artificial companion, especially when the technology is still in its formative stages. This suggests that for investors and product developers, a strategic focus on problem-solving, utility-driven AI with robust ethical frameworks, particularly in areas like hands-free assistance and content capture, will likely yield more immediate and sustainable success than ventures into ethically complex or technically immature domains.

data.title: AI’s Market Ascent: Beyond the Fed’s Shadow
data.description:
The current market narrative is a complex interplay between the transformative promise of artificial intelligence and the looming shadow of central bank monetary policy. While the Federal Reserve’s future actions often dominate financial headlines, the underlying structural shifts driven by AI are increasingly dictating where true value is being created and captured. This dynamic tension was a central theme in a recent CNBC Worldwide Exchange discussion featuring William Lee, Chief Economist at the Milken Institute, and Storm Uru, Global Innovation Team Co-Head at Liontrust Asset Management, as they dissected the primary drivers of market action.

Dom Chu, the host, steered the conversation towards the pivotal question: is the AI boom or the Fed the biggest driver of market action? Their insights provided a nuanced perspective for founders, venture capitalists, and AI professionals navigating this unprecedented economic landscape.

William Lee articulated a clear consensus regarding the Federal Reserve’s trajectory. He stated, “I don’t think there’s any debate at all that the Fed is going to be easing,” underscoring a widespread expectation among economists and market participants. The true uncertainty, he clarified, lies not in the direction, but in the pace and timing of these cuts, driven by the varying data interpretations and perspectives among FOMC members. Despite differing hawkish or dovish leanings, Lee observed a universal agreement that the neutral rate is below current levels, and all FOMC members aspire to reach that equilibrium. This suggests a fundamental belief that current rates are restrictive, implying a future path towards lower borrowing costs, regardless of immediate data fluctuations.

Transitioning to the AI phenomenon, Lee highlighted a critical divergence in market perception. He pointed to the contrasting fortunes of tech giants like Google (Alphabet) and Nvidia. Google, with its new Gemini 3 model, presented clearer “visibility of cash flow,” which investors are gravitating towards. Conversely, Nvidia, a bellwether for AI infrastructure, faces questions regarding its long-term infrastructure trade, suggesting a more speculative or cyclical component to its valuation. For Lee, this distinction led to a provocative conclusion: “the markets are more concerned about the AI trade than the Fed,” assuming the consensus on easing holds true.

Storm Uru of Liontrust Asset Management echoed the sentiment of AI’s profound impact, characterizing it as “without a doubt a very large structural shift.” He pointed to the staggering scale of investment, with projections of $3 to $4 trillion in AI infrastructure buildout by 2030. This monumental capital expenditure, according to Uru, signifies a significant reorientation of the technology sector’s value chain. He also noted an important reset in market sentiment over recent weeks, presenting timely opportunities for investors to build positions in key enablers of this shift.

This structural shift, Uru contended, extends far beyond the well-known “Magnificent Seven” tech giants. While these large players often fund the initial capital expenditure, the true economic profit is “shifting down into the infrastructure layer from the software layer.” This insight is crucial for investors looking beyond the immediate headlines, identifying companies that provide the foundational “picks and shovels” for the AI revolution. Applied Materials, which supplies equipment to manufacturers like TSMC for advanced silicon production, exemplifies this opportunity. As the industry transitions to smaller, more energy-efficient 2-nanometer nodes, companies like Applied Materials are positioned to be significant beneficiaries over the next three years, enabling the next generation of high-performing chips for industry leaders such as Apple, Nvidia, and AMD.

Despite the excitement surrounding AI, the broader economic picture presents considerable challenges that even the Fed must address. Lee emphasized that current rates are too high relative to the underlying economic activity. While headline GDP growth figures might appear robust, much of this is “skewed toward capital spending” in the AI infrastructure trade. The rest of the economy, particularly consumer spending, is not as strong. This is evidenced by a stalling labor market, with employment growth concentrated in lower-wage service sectors like healthcare and retail, while high-wage industries experience layoffs and mergers and acquisitions. The Fed’s concern over this uneven economic strength, coupled with persistent inflation, creates a complex decision-making environment for future rate adjustments. Lee stressed that rate cuts should occur quickly due to the approximately year-to-year-and-a-half lag in monetary policy’s impact.

Related Reading

  • AI's Enduring Rally: Navigating Growth and Diversification in a Broadening Market
  • AI to Drive Market Higher, Deflate Costs Through 2026
  • Google's AI Chip Ascent Signals Healthy Competition, Not Bubble Burst

Uru’s biggest fear for 2026 underscores this macro vulnerability: “inflation returning and long-end interest rates coming up.” Such a scenario would have a detrimental impact on longer-duration assets and the very AI infrastructure buildout that currently excites investors. The confluence of these factors—the need for rate cuts to support the broader economy, the potential for persistent inflation, and the fiscal challenges of a massive deficit—paints a picture of an economy walking a tightrope. The market’s current fixation on AI’s growth potential might be obscuring these underlying macro risks, creating a precarious balance for the coming years. Furthermore, Lee expressed little optimism for legislative action from Congress to address the perennial fiscal deficit, implying that any potential relief would have to come from revenue growth, which itself remains uncertain.

Ultimately, the conversation highlighted that while AI represents a profound technological and economic transformation, its trajectory remains intertwined with the broader macroeconomic environment. The Fed’s actions, while seemingly predictable in direction, still carry significant weight in terms of timing and magnitude, impacting the cost of capital for this massive AI buildout. For leaders in the startup ecosystem, VCs, and AI professionals, understanding this dual influence—the structural shift of AI and the cyclical forces of monetary policy—is paramount to navigating the opportunities and risks that lie ahead.

data.title: AI Capex Fuels 2026 Market Outlook, Broader Participation Hinges on Rate Cuts
data.description:
The trajectory of artificial intelligence capital expenditure is poised to be a significant driver for market performance through 2026, a central theme explored in a recent CNBC discussion. Gina Sanchez, CEO of Chantico Global and chief market strategist at Lido Advisors, engaged with a CNBC anchor to dissect the market roadmap for 2026, deliberating whether AI investment or shifts in monetary policy would be the primary catalyst for stock gains. Their conversation offered a nuanced perspective for founders, venture capitalists, and AI professionals navigating the evolving economic landscape.

Sanchez laid out her firm’s core assumption for the coming years, stating plainly, “Our 2026 market roadmap does assume that we continue to see significant capex spending into the infrastructure of AI.” This capital infusion is not merely a transient trend but a sustained commitment expected to bolster the “picks and shovels” companies within the AI ecosystem. Firms like Nvidia, Microsoft, and Alphabet are positioned as key beneficiaries, underpinning the continued growth of the AI infrastructure. The sheer scale of this investment, with major tech players committing billions, suggests a foundational shift rather than a speculative bubble, indicating that the core technology providers remain compelling long-term plays despite their current valuations.

While the “Magnificent Seven” dominated market gains in 2025, Sanchez anticipates a broader market participation in 2026. This shift is critical for a healthier, more diversified market performance. The concentration of gains in a handful of mega-cap tech stocks has been a point of concern for many analysts, but the expectation of a “broadening out” suggests a more inclusive growth environment.

This broader market engagement, however, is contingent upon a crucial external factor: the re-opening of the IPO and M&A markets. Sanchez emphasized, “One of those two things needs to happen. We don’t have to have both, but we need one of them.” The dormancy in these markets has stifled liquidity and growth opportunities for smaller, innovative companies, which are often the engines of new economic cycles. A resurgence in IPOs and M&A activity would unlock capital, stimulate competition, and allow a wider array of companies, including small-cap firms that have been “in the mud” for several years, to gain traction and contribute to overall market buoyancy.

The catalyst for unlocking these dormant markets, according to Sanchez, is a shift in monetary policy. Specifically, she points to “an expectation of rate cuts in 2026” as essential for spurring broader market sentiment and enabling M&A and IPO activity. The current high-interest rate environment has made borrowing more expensive, dampening the appetite for new public offerings and large-scale acquisitions. A dovish pivot by the Federal Reserve, supported by economic data, would lower the cost of capital, making it more attractive for companies to go public, for larger entities to acquire smaller innovators, and for venture capital to find more viable exit strategies. This is a critical insight for VCs and founders, as the availability of capital and exit opportunities directly impacts the startup ecosystem’s vitality.

Despite the prevailing economic headwinds that might justify rate cuts, Sanchez maintains an optimistic outlook on corporate earnings. She expects earnings to “continue to be quite good” in 2026, suggesting that companies, particularly those benefiting from AI infrastructure build-out, possess inherent strength that transcends some broader economic concerns. This resilience in earnings, coupled with potential rate cuts, could create a fertile ground for investment. The market strategist highlighted that many companies within the broader tech sector, including Microsoft, Google, and Meta, are “very, very reasonably priced” when considering the ongoing significant investments in AI. This underscores an opportunity for strategic investment in established players that are actively shaping the future of AI.

Related Reading

  • Epoch AI Unveils Frontier Data Centers Hub: Mapping AI's Monumental Infrastructure
  • AI's Insatiable Power Hunger Redefines Data Center Infrastructure
  • AI Shifts from Build-Out to Utilization: A New Investment Landscape

However, Sanchez cautioned against a blanket approach, advising investors to be “very picky” about their choices. While some large tech firms offer value, others, like Palantir, may not be as favorably priced. This discernment is vital for founders and investors seeking to capitalize on the AI trend without succumbing to overvalued assets. The real estate sector was also identified as an “interesting space” where rate cuts could alleviate current frustrations, potentially offering another avenue for investment as market conditions normalize.

Ultimately, the 2026 market outlook is a complex interplay of sustained AI capital expenditure, the re-invigoration of IPO and M&A markets, and the anticipated easing of monetary policy. These factors, if they align as predicted, could usher in a period of broader market participation and robust earnings, moving beyond the concentrated gains of recent years.

data.title: ASEAN’s Digital Sovereignty: Building, Not Buying, the AI Future
data.description:
The digital future, for nations and regions alike, hinges on a fundamental choice: to build or to buy. This critical strategic imperative was underscored by Yeoh Keong Yeen, Executive Director of YTL Construction, during his commentary at the Bloomberg Business Summit at ASEAN. His insights illuminate a pivotal moment for the Southeast Asian bloc, advocating for a profound shift from being a mere consumer of technology to becoming a robust producer, especially in the burgeoning fields of artificial intelligence, digital infrastructure, and green transitions.

Yeoh Keong Yeen, representing YTL Construction, spoke at the Bloomberg Business Summit at ASEAN about the region’s vast potential and the strategic importance of developing indigenous AI capabilities. His perspective offers a clear roadmap for how ASEAN, with its significant population and rapid digitization, can leverage its unique position to foster resilience and independence in the global technology landscape. The discussion revolved around the necessity of investing in foundational AI infrastructure, from data centers to large language models, to secure a competitive edge and safeguard national interests.

ASEAN stands at a unique confluence of demographic advantage and global supply chain realignment. With a population of 680 million, one of the youngest and fastest digitizing in the world, the region offers an unparalleled testbed for scalable, localized technology. Yeoh articulated this opportunity with precision, stating, “ASEAN’s biggest opportunities is the shift from being a consumer market to becoming a producer of technologies, especially in AI, digital infrastructure, and green transitions.” This isn’t merely an economic aspiration; it’s a strategic imperative for long-term growth and self-determination. For founders and VCs, this signals a fertile ground for investment in deep tech, where local solutions for local problems, built on sovereign infrastructure, can yield substantial returns and create enduring value.

The core of Yeoh’s argument centers on the concept of “sovereign AI.” This philosophy transcends mere technological adoption, delving into the very fabric of national security, economic resilience, and cultural preservation. He posits that true independence in the digital age requires control over the underlying intelligence systems that will power future economies and societies. Relying solely on foreign-built AI models, data centers, and compute infrastructure creates dependencies that can be strategically vulnerable. The implications for defense and AI analysts are clear: digital sovereignty is increasingly intertwined with national security, making the development of localized AI capabilities a matter of strategic defense.

YTL Construction, under Yeoh’s leadership, is actively demonstrating this “build, not buy” philosophy in Malaysia. The company is investing in a full-stack sovereign AI ecosystem, a comprehensive approach that spans the entire technological spectrum. This includes developing green-powered data centers, establishing AI cloud infrastructure in partnership with NVIDIA utilizing their latest GB200 chips, and critically, creating Malaysia’s own sovereign large language model (LLM) called Ilmu. Ilmu is not designed to compete directly with global AI giants but rather to serve the specific linguistic, cultural, and national interests of Malaysia, ensuring that the intelligence powering its society is contextually relevant and securely managed.

This integrated approach to AI development is a blueprint for other ASEAN nations. By owning the entire stack—from the physical infrastructure and compute power to the data and the trained models—nations can mitigate risks associated with data privacy, algorithmic bias, and potential foreign interference. Such independence fosters a vibrant local innovation ecosystem, creating high-value jobs and nurturing a talent pool that understands the unique nuances of the region. It ensures that the benefits of AI development accrue domestically, strengthening national economies and preventing capital flight.

Related Reading

  • Epoch AI Unveils Frontier Data Centers Hub: Mapping AI's Monumental Infrastructure
  • AI's Insatiable Power Hunger Redefines Data Center Infrastructure

The strategic importance of this endeavor cannot be overstated. “The economies that build, not just buy, will lead,” Yeoh declared, encapsulating the competitive edge gained through self-reliance. This is a call to action for regional leaders and investors to prioritize indigenous development over passive consumption. It’s about cultivating an environment where local talent can thrive, where data remains within national borders, and where the intelligence that drives progress is tailored to local needs and values.

This paradigm shift unlocks not just innovation but also profound economic competitiveness. It fosters digital independence, moving beyond reliance on external systems that may not align with national priorities. Ultimately, this leads to long-term strategic resilience for the entire ASEAN region, allowing member states to navigate future geopolitical and technological shifts with greater autonomy and confidence. The vision presented is one where ASEAN nations collectively forge their own digital destiny, building a shared foundation of technological strength and sovereignty.

data.title: Agentic Commerce Isn’t Ready, But AI Agents Are Everywhere
data.description:
“I don’t think this is a finite game, I don’t think there is a winner. I think this is the classic Simon Sinek infinite game. I think the players are going to play until they run out of resources and they can no longer play the game.” This assertion from Chris Hay, a Distinguished Engineer, set a provocative tone for the “Mixture of Experts” podcast’s Thanksgiving special, which gathered industry leaders to dissect the hype and reality of AI agents. Host Tim Hwang, alongside Hay, Lauren McHugh Olende (Program Director, AI Open Innovation), and Volkmar Uhlig (VP Core AI and WatsonX.ai), debated whether 2025 truly marks the “year of agents,” particularly in the realm of agentic commerce. Their collective insights painted a picture of a rapidly evolving landscape, where breakthrough AI capabilities are emerging, yet significant hurdles remain for widespread, consumer-facing applications.

The conversation kicked off with a deep dive into Anthropic’s new Claude 4.5 Opus model, a recent entrant that has quickly impressed experts. Mihai Criveti, another Distinguished Engineer specializing in Agentic AI, lauded its efficiency. “It’s 50% more efficient than Claude Opus 4.1… it’s one of the most efficient models out there,” Criveti stated, highlighting its superior performance for coding tasks, even surpassing recent releases from competitors like Google and OpenAI. This rapid iteration and specialization of models underscore the intense competition and breakneck pace of development within the AI space. The immediate impact of such models, particularly in code generation and testing, signals a significant shift in developer workflows.

However, the enthusiasm tempered when the discussion shifted to consumer-facing agentic commerce, especially the prospect of it dominating shopping events like Black Friday 2025. Chris Hay was unequivocal in his skepticism. “No, I don’t think it will be,” he declared, suggesting that the industry is “probably another year away” from such a breakthrough. While companies like OpenAI are integrating shopping capabilities and partnering with platforms like Shopify, the sheer number of retailers yet to be onboarded, coupled with geographical limitations (currently US-only), indicates a nascent stage of development. Google’s agentic commerce protocol faces similar early-stage challenges.

The true impact of agents, according to Volkmar Uhlig, lies not in direct consumer interaction, but in the backend. He argued, “I think the agents are not the consumer-facing agents, but the agents are actually the backend.” Uhlig pointed to existing optimizations in areas like returns processing—a significant pain point for retailers, especially after major shopping holidays. These agentic workflows, though invisible to the end-user, are already streamlining operations and reducing labor costs for major retailers. This distinction is critical: while consumers may not yet interact with sophisticated shopping agents, businesses are increasingly leveraging AI behind the scenes.

Lauren McHugh Olende echoed this sentiment, questioning the perceived value of simply automating the checkout process. “I don’t see a big revolution coming from the simple act of being able to check it out once you’re in the AI application,” she noted. She emphasized that the real challenge lies in the quality of product research and matching capabilities within AI models. Current general-purpose chatbots often fall short in understanding nuanced customer intentions or specific product attributes like size, dimensions, or style. This highlights a need for AI models trained specifically on vast datasets of e-commerce interactions and product information to truly enhance the shopping experience beyond basic automation.

The developer ecosystem for building these agents is vibrant, with numerous tools and frameworks emerging. However, the path from prototype to production remains arduous. Uhlig articulated this challenge, stating, “The Shopify moment for agents hasn’t happened.” This refers to the lack of an easily accessible, low-code platform that democratizes agent development and deployment for small to medium-sized businesses. Current agent development still requires a high degree of technical expertise, necessitating complex orchestration of multiple models and external tools.

Related Reading

  • AI Agents Transform Holiday Shopping into a Transactional Battleground
  • AI to Drive Market Higher, Deflate Costs Through 2026
  • AI Shifts from Build-Out to Utilization: A New Investment Landscape

The complexity of managing agents, ensuring they stay “on track” and don’t “go off the rails,” is another significant hurdle. Agents often need guardrails, deterministic flows, and robust planning modules to execute tasks reliably, especially in real-world consumer scenarios where unpredictable inputs are common. This demands sophisticated frameworks and careful integration, moving beyond simple API calls to a more structured and controlled environment. The current state is akin to a “baby programmer interface,” as Uhlig described it, suitable for those who can code but not yet for mass adoption by non-developers.

Despite these challenges, the panelists agreed that agents are indeed here, albeit in a less glamorous, more infrastructural role than often portrayed. They are woven into existing digital experiences, often unnoticed by the end-user. The future of agentic commerce, therefore, hinges on making these powerful tools more accessible, more specialized for retail, and more capable of reliably executing complex, multi-step tasks without constant human oversight. The market is ripe for innovation that bridges this gap between advanced AI capabilities and seamless, dependable commercial application.

data.title: AIE Code Summit: The Unspoken Rhythms of AI Innovation
data.description:
The driving, synthesized soundscapes that provided the backdrop for the AIE Code Summit are more than mere ambiance; they are the pulsating heartbeat of an industry in constant flux. While this particular release is a compilation of instrumental tracks, explicitly offered “by popular demand” from the livestream and venue stage, the very essence of such an event speaks volumes about the current state and future trajectory of artificial intelligence. It underscores a community deeply engaged not just with theoretical advancements, but with the practical, code-level challenges that define the next generation of intelligent systems.

This collection of instrumental tracks, explicitly released “by popular demand” from the AIE Code Summit livestream and venue stage, provides an auditory backdrop to what was undoubtedly a critical gathering for AI engineers and developers. While the video itself presents no direct dialogue, the very nature of an “AI Engineer Code Summit” suggests a forum where leading minds converged to dissect the intricacies of advanced algorithms, machine learning frameworks, and the ethical considerations shaping the future of intelligent systems. The music, ranging from high-energy electronic beats to more reflective, ambient passages, mirrors the diverse demands and intense focus inherent in cutting-edge AI development.

The choice of instrumental electronic music for such a summit is itself a statement. It avoids distraction, allowing for concentrated thought, yet provides a constant undercurrent of energy and forward momentum. This sonic tapestry likely accompanied countless hours of focused coding, collaborative problem-solving, and the deep dives into complex datasets that characterize the daily grind of AI engineering. It’s a subtle reinforcement of the idea that, even in moments of intense intellectual labor, there is an underlying current of excitement and purpose.

The summit, implicitly, was a crucible for innovation. Here, engineers likely wrestled with optimizing large language models, designing robust autonomous systems, or perhaps integrating AI into defense applications – fields where precision and performance are paramount. The repetitive yet evolving patterns in the music could symbolize the iterative nature of development, where small, persistent changes lead to significant breakthroughs. It’s a reminder that progress in AI is rarely a single flash of genius, but rather the cumulative effort of dedicated teams.

One core insight from the event’s very existence is the accelerating demand for specialized AI engineering talent. The fact that organizers felt compelled to host a “Code Summit” specifically for AI engineers highlights a growing recognition that this is a distinct and crucial discipline, demanding dedicated platforms for knowledge exchange and skill refinement. As AI moves beyond academic research into pervasive real-world applications, the need for practitioners who can translate complex models into stable, scalable, and secure systems becomes paramount. The market for these individuals is fiercely competitive, and events like this serve as vital talent magnets and incubators for expertise.

The music further suggests an environment where the stakes are high. The driving basslines and intricate synth melodies evoke the rapid pace of technological evolution, where staying ahead means constant learning and adaptation. Founders and venture capitalists understand that the speed of execution in AI is often a differentiator, and a summit steeped in such energetic, focused sound likely fostered an ethos of agile development and rapid iteration. The very atmosphere, curated through sound, speaks to the urgency of building and deploying robust AI solutions.

Beyond the technical, the summit implicitly touched upon the broader implications of AI. While the focus was on code, the underlying discussions would inevitably drift towards responsible AI, data privacy, and the societal impact of powerful new technologies. The subtle shifts in the music, from intense bursts to more contemplative tones, could have marked transitions between hardcore coding sessions and broader, more philosophical discussions. This duality reflects the industry’s growing awareness of its immense power and the ethical responsibility that accompanies it.

Related Reading

  • Google Cloud's Veo Redefines AI Video Generation with Unprecedented Realism and Control
  • Google Cloud's Veo Model Unleashes Granular Control in AI Video Editing
  • Google Cloud's Gemini Image Redefines AI-Powered Creative Workflows

The very act of releasing these instrumentals, as the organizers stated, was “by popular demand.” This singular phrase, drawn directly from the video’s accompanying text, serves as a powerful testament to the community’s engagement and the perceived value of the summit’s atmosphere. It suggests that the attendees found not just technical value, but an almost visceral connection to the event’s energy, a feeling they wished to recreate. It underscores the human element in a field often characterized by algorithms and data points.

The AIE Code Summit, even in its silent, instrumental form, paints a vivid picture of a dynamic and critical sector. It’s a testament to the engineers who are not just writing code, but actively shaping the future, one algorithm, one line of code, one driving beat at a time. The sound is an invitation to founders, VCs, and AI professionals to lean in and listen to the underlying currents of innovation.
